questions.question_text: A developer creates a planned community in Minnesota in 2024. Which statute governs this development?
distractors: Chapter 515, original condominium statute
Chapter 515A, the Uniform Condo Act
Chapter 500, the Uniform Planned Community Act
questions.explanation: Chapter 515B, the Minnesota Common Interest Ownership Act, governs condos, co-ops, townhomes, and planned communities created after June 1, 1994. A 2024 planned community falls under this statute.

questions.question_text: An HOA board approves a special assessment for roof repairs without owner input. Which governance requirement may have been violated?
distractors: State licensing of the property manager
County building permit requirements
Individual unit owner consent for all repairs
questions.explanation: Under the MN CIO Act, the board of directors must follow proper budget approval procedures for assessments. Special assessments typically require notice and may require owner approval depending on the governing documents.

questions.question_text: A condominium owner in a CIO fails to pay a special assessment. What is the association's primary remedy?
questions.correct_answer: The unpaid assessment becomes a lien on the unit
distractors: The association may evict the owner immediately
The board can transfer the unit to another buyer
The owner loses voting rights but owes nothing
questions.explanation: Under Minnesota law, unpaid assessments in a common interest community are liens on the individual unit. This gives the association the ability to enforce collection, including potential foreclosure of the lien.

questions.question_text: Which body within a CIO is primarily responsible for managing the community's finances and operations?
distractors: County housing authority
Minnesota Commerce Department
Individual unit owners collectively
questions.explanation: The board of directors is responsible for day-to-day governance of a common interest community, including budgets, reserves, assessments, and maintenance of common elements.

questions.question_text: A buyer is comparing two Minnesota condominiums. One was built in 1985 and the other in 2010. How do the governing statutes differ?
questions.correct_answer: The 1985 condo uses Ch. 515A; the 2010 uses Ch. 515B
distractors: Both are governed by Ch. 515B regardless of creation date
The 1985 condo uses Ch. 515; the 2010 uses Ch. 515A
The 1985 condo is fully exempt from all CIO statutes
questions.explanation: Chapter 515A, the Uniform Condominium Act, governs condominiums created after August 1, 1980, while the original condominium act (Ch. 515) governs condos created before that date. Common interest communities created on or after June 1, 1994 fall under Ch. 515B, the Minnesota Common Interest Ownership Act, and certain 515B provisions also apply to older condominiums.

questions.question_text: A seller in a Minnesota CIO community must provide what document to the buyer before closing?
distractors: Title insurance commitment only
County property tax statement
Builder warranty transfer form
questions.explanation: Minnesota law requires sellers in common interest communities to provide a resale disclosure certificate before closing. This document discloses financial and governance information about the association.

questions.question_text: After receiving CIO disclosures, a buyer decides the association's financial condition is too risky. What right does the buyer have?
questions.correct_answer: Right of rescission after receiving disclosures
distractors: Right to demand the association increase reserves
Right to renegotiate the purchase price only
No cancellation rights once an offer is accepted
questions.explanation: Minnesota law grants CIO buyers a right of rescission after receiving the required disclosures. This allows buyers to cancel the transaction if the association's condition is unsatisfactory.

questions.question_text: A CIO association in Minnesota has not set aside money for future roof replacement. Which requirement is being violated?
distractors: Requirement to carry flood insurance
Duty to conduct annual unit inspections
State mandate for annual independent audits
questions.explanation: Minnesota CIO law requires associations to maintain adequate reserves for common elements such as roofs, siding, and parking areas. Failing to reserve funds violates this statutory obligation.

questions.question_text: A buyer reviews a resale disclosure certificate and discovers the CIO has pending litigation and underfunded reserves. The buyer wants to cancel. Which analysis is correct?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer may rescind based on the disclosure right
distractors: The buyer can cancel only if litigation exceeds $50,000
Rescission is available only for new construction
The seller must cure the deficiency before closing
questions.explanation: The right of rescission applies after receiving CIO disclosures regardless of the specific dollar amount of issues. The buyer may exercise this right based on any information revealed in the certificate.

questions.question_text: Which statement about the CIO resale disclosure certificate is accurate?
questions.correct_answer: It must be provided before closing on a resale
distractors: It is optional if the buyer waives it in writing
It applies only to condominiums, not townhomes
The county recorder prepares and certifies it
questions.explanation: The resale disclosure certificate is a mandatory document that must be provided before closing. It cannot be waived, applies to all CIO types under Ch. 515B, and is prepared by the association.

questions.question_text: A homeowner discovers a leaking pipe 18 months after moving into a newly built home. Under Ch. 327A.02, which warranty applies?
distractors: One-year warranty for faulty workmanship
Ten-year warranty for structural defects
No warranty because the one-year period expired
questions.explanation: Minnesota's statutory home warranty (Minn. Stat. 327A.02) covers defects caused by faulty installation of plumbing, electrical, heating, and cooling systems for two years from the warranty date. A leak discovered at 18 months falls within this two-year systems warranty.

questions.question_text: When does the statutory home warranty period begin under Minnesota law?
questions.correct_answer: The earlier of first occupancy or the initial buyer taking title
distractors: Date the building permit was issued
Date the certificate of occupancy was granted
One year after substantial completion of construction
questions.explanation: Under Minn. Stat. 327A.01, subd. 8, the "warranty date" is the earliest of (a) the initial vendee's first occupancy of the dwelling or (b) the date the initial vendee takes legal or equitable title. All three statutory warranty periods — one, two, and ten years — run from that date.

questions.question_text: A builder's contract includes a clause stating the buyer waives all statutory warranty rights. Is this enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: No, statutory home warranties cannot be waived
distractors: Yes, blanket waivers are enforceable if the buyer initials the clause
Yes, waivers are valid on homes priced over $500,000
Only if both parties are represented by attorneys
questions.explanation: Under Minn. Stat. 327A.04, subd. 1, a contract clause waiving the statutory warranties is void. Only narrow exceptions exist: a specific warranty may be excluded or modified through a separate signed instrument in boldface type in which the builder provides substitute express warranties offering substantially the same protections (subd. 2), or by a recorded waiver covering one specific, disclosed major construction defect with a corresponding price reduction (subd. 3). A blanket waiver of all warranty rights satisfies neither exception.

questions.question_text: A second owner discovers a major foundation crack 8 years after the home was first occupied. Does the statutory warranty apply?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the 10-year structural warranty runs with the property
distractors: No, warranties expire when the first owner sells
Only if the builder agrees to extend coverage
No, foundation issues are excluded from coverage
questions.explanation: The 10-year warranty for major structural defects runs with the property to subsequent purchasers. Since the foundation crack appeared within 10 years of first occupancy, the second owner is covered.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT covered by Minnesota's statutory home warranty?
questions.correct_answer: Normal wear and tear on existing homes
distractors: Faulty workmanship within one year
Major structural defects within ten years
HVAC system failures within two years
questions.explanation: The statutory warranty does not cover wear and tear, homeowner-caused damage, or existing homes. It applies only to new construction and covers workmanship (1 year), systems (2 years), and structure (10 years).
